items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: The brain and spinal cord together form the:
items.why: Correct answer: CNS. “PNS” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Fight-or-flight is triggered by which nervous system branch?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sympathetic. “Parasympathetic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many lobes does each cerebral hemisphere have?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 lobes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
